prompt: Two pairs of rice accessions are presented with both ADMIXTURE ancestry proportions (K=5) and KING-robust kinship coefficients. Pair A has very similar ancestry proportions but moderate kinship. Pair B has dissimilar ancestry proportions but lower kinship. Which pair is more closely related, and why do ancestry proportions fail to predict kinship?
context:
Pair A:
Sample            Comp_1(ind)  Comp_2(trj)  Comp_3(tej)  Comp_4(aus)  Comp_5(aro)  F
IRIS_313-8695     0.86         0.02         0.01         0.08         0.03         0.775
IRIS_313-9318     0.88         0.01         0.01         0.07         0.03         0.764
KINSHIP = 0.164  [Second_Degree]

Pair B:
Sample            Comp_1(ind)  Comp_2(trj)  Comp_3(tej)  Comp_4(aus)  Comp_5(aro)  F
IRIS_313-9102     0.82         0.03         0.02         0.10         0.03         0.749
IRIS_313-7501     0.14         0.06         0.04         0.42         0.34         0.812
KINSHIP = 0.094  [Second_Degree]

KING-robust kinship thresholds:
  > 0.354  = Duplicate/MZ twin
  0.177-0.354 = First-degree
  0.0884-0.177 = Second-degree
  0.0442-0.0884 = Third-degree

Note: Both pairs are classified as second-degree, but Pair A's kinship (0.164) is near the first-degree boundary while Pair B's (0.094) is near the third-degree boundary.
correct_answer: Pair A (kinship=0.164) is more closely related than Pair B (kinship=0.094), despite Pair A having very similar ancestry proportions and Pair B having dissimilar ones. This illustrates a fundamental distinction between ancestry proportions and kinship. ADMIXTURE Q-values reflect population-of-origin composition -- the proportion of an individual's genome that traces back to each ancestral subpopulation. Two unrelated indica accessions will have similar Q-values simply because they were drawn from the same subpopulation, not because they share recent ancestors. Kinship (KING-robust) measures identity-by-descent (IBD) -- the proportion of the genome inherited from a specific recent common ancestor. Pair A shares a recent ancestor (half-sibs or avuncular) even though their similar indica ancestry proportions could be coincidental. Pair B has different ancestry proportions (one is indica, the other is aus/aromatic admixed), indicating they come from different subpopulations, yet they still share some recent ancestry (kinship=0.094), possibly from a breeding program that crossed indica with aus/aromatic germplasm. The practical lesson: population ancestry proportions describe where genome segments originated, while kinship describes whether two individuals inherited specific segments from the same recent source. They measure different timescales and should not be used interchangeably.
rubric: Full marks (3): Correctly identifies Pair A as more closely related (higher kinship), clearly distinguishes ancestry proportions (population-of-origin) from kinship (recent IBD sharing), explains why similar Q-values do not imply close relatedness, and explains why different Q-values do not preclude relatedness. Mostly correct (2): Identifies Pair A as more closely related and explains the distinction, but does not fully articulate why similar ancestry proportions arise in unrelated individuals. Partial (1): Correctly identifies which pair is more related but conflates ancestry and kinship. Incorrect (0): Identifies Pair B as more closely related. Major error: Stating that ancestry proportions predict kinship.
notes: Tests understanding of the fundamental difference between population ancestry and individual relatedness in rice.
